headline: The M4's bf16 matrix instruction (BFDOT/BFMMLA) buys SPEED, not accuracy. Exact fp64 oracle: BFDOT is ~6.8x faster than a naive scalar bf16->fp32 loop, but its accuracy 'win' over that loop is ENTIRELY accumulator-count (BFDOT uses 4 fp32 lanes, the naive scalar uses 1) -- a fair matched-lane 4-accumulator SOFTWARE fp32 loop is 1.2-1.3x MORE accurate than BFDOT at K>=256 (hw/naive4=1.24@4096). So ARM's fused bf16 rounding is slightly WORSE than plain fp32 accumulation; the multi-lane reduction tree is the only accuracy lever and software gets it for free. Bit-nondeterministic under regrouping (fp32 non-associative, ~4e-6). Takeaway: use bf16 matrix instructions for THROUGHPUT; for accuracy a 4-lane fp32 loop (or Kahan, flat ~2.6e-8) beats them.

P1_hw_more_accurate.note: accuracy edge is accumulator-count not bf16-hardware; ARM fused rounding slightly hurts vs matched-lane fp32

P2_bit_determinism.note: NOT deterministic (~4e-6 low bits); positive control live

P3_error_growth.note: naive4 grows same rate as hw -> the multi-accumulator tree, not bf16, sets the growth

P4_throughput.note: vs naive SCALAR fp32; vectorized fp32 would narrow it

P5_fp16_placement.note: fp16 reference NOT implemented (deferred); only falsifier verified (bf16-hw does not beat fp32/Kahan). Order: Kahan-fp32 < naive4-fp32 < BFDOT-hw < naive1-seq-bf16

asm_audit: 15 bfdot/bfmmla emitted; naive is a genuine scalar sequential fp32 chain (asm-confirmed)
bfmmla_scope: numerics via BFDOT (the bf16 dot under test); BFMMLA gate-verified ([10 20 26 52] exact), same fp32 accumulator+rounding; BFMMLA GEMM EXPECTED (not measured) non-deterministic by the same non-associativity; full BFMMLA sweep a stated extension
